management_principles.overview: The management of thyroid carcinoma is guided by histologic type (differentiated [papillary, follicular, oncocytic], medullary, anaplastic), risk stratification (clinicopathologic features, age, tumor size, extension, nodal status, metastases), and response to initial therapy. Differentiated thyroid carcinomas (DTC) have excellent prognosis with 10-year survival exceeding 90-95% [MS-2]. Anaplastic carcinoma is nearly uniformly fatal. Surgery is the primary treatment for most thyroid cancers, with radioactive iodine (RAI) for selected DTC cases, and TSH suppression therapy. For advanced RAI-refractory DTC and medullary carcinoma, kinase inhibitors (lenvatinib, sorafenib, cabozantinib, vandetanib) and targeted therapies (selpercatinib, pralsetinib, larotrectinib) are used. Anaplastic carcinoma requires expedited multidisciplinary care, often with neoadjuvant targeted therapy, surgery, chemoradiation, and palliative measures. The NCCN panel emphasizes a risk-adapted approach and dynamic risk assessment [MS-12-MS-13]. Systemic therapy is reserved for progressive/symptomatic disease, not for stable indolent disease [THYR-B 4 of 5]. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise noted.

by_intent.approach: Surgery (lobectomy or total thyroidectomy) ± RAI ablation/adjuvant therapy ± TSH suppression
Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection, ± lateral neck dissection, ± prophylactic thyroidectomy for germline RET carriers
Consider neoadjuvant targeted therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E) then surgery, adjuvant chemoradiation

by_intent.patient_group: Advanced RAI-refractory DTC, medullary, anaplastic with distant metastases
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (targeted, immunotherapy), local therapies (RT, ablation, embolization), best supportive care

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary team management is strongly recommended, especially for anaplastic carcinoma (expedient consultation) and for bulky locoregional disease [PAP-3]. Team should include endocrinology, surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, nuclear medicine, pathology, and palliative care. Referral to high-volume centers is advised for anaplastic and complex cases [ONC-2A].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (ECOG) is considered when selecting systemic therapy; patients with ECOG PS 0 had improved PFS and OS with lenvatinib compared to ECOG PS 1 [MS-27]. For kinase inhibitor therapy, the pace of disease progression and side effect burden must be balanced against quality of life [THYR-B 4 of 5].

management_pathways.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma – Classic Subtype, Low Risk
branching_basis: Tumor size ≤2 cm (or ≤1 cm for microcarcinoma)
Unifocal or multifocal with all foci ≤1 cm

nodes.criteria: All low-risk factors present; patient candidate for active surveillance or surgery

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: For papillary microcarcinoma (≤1 cm) without suspicious nodes or invasion, active surveillance with neck ultrasound every 6 months for 1-2 years then annually is appropriate (THYR-D).

options.label: Lobectomy + isthmusectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2B for total thyroidectomy; lobectomy is preferred for low-risk tumors 1-4 cm (PAP-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI not typically recommended. No adjuvant therapy needed. TSH suppression to normal range (THYR-A).

management_pathways.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma – Intermediate Risk (RAI Selectively Recommended)
branching_basis: High-risk subtypes (tall cell, columnar, hobnail, diffuse sclerosing, solid/trabecular)
Cervical lymph node metastases (millimetric central nodes)

nodes.criteria: Any of the above intermediate-risk factors after total thyroidectomy

options.label: Total thyroidectomy (if not already done)
options.recommendation: Completion thyroidectomy for those who had lobectomy with intermediate-risk features (PAP-2).

options.label: RAI therapy
options.recommendation: Empiric dose of 30-50 mCi for remnant ablation (category 1) or 75-150 mCi for adjuvant therapy based on clinicopathologic factors (PAP-4, PAP-5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI is selectively recommended. TSH suppression to 0.1-0.5 mU/L for patients with biochemical evidence of disease but no structural disease; otherwise to normal range if disease-free after several years (THYR-A).

management_pathways.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma – High Risk (RAI Typically Recommended)
branching_basis: Differentiated high-grade carcinoma (≥5 mitoses/2 mm² and/or tumor necrosis)
Known or suspected distant metastases at presentation

nodes.criteria: Any high-risk factor after total thyroidectomy

options.label: RAI therapy
options.recommendation: Dose 100-200 mCi for known disease; consider dosimetry for large-volume disease. Post-therapy whole body scan performed (THYR-C 2 of 5).

options.label: Resect clinically significant structural disease if possible
options.recommendation: Before RAI, resect any significant gross residual neck disease (PAP-5).

options.label: EBRT
options.recommendation: For unresectable gross residual disease threatening vital structures, or for locoregional invasive disease (PAP-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI is typically recommended. TSH suppression to <0.1 mU/L for patients with known structural residual disease or high risk of recurrence (THYR-A).

management_pathways.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma – RAI-Refractory Locally Recurrent/Advanced/Metastatic
branching_basis: RAI-refractory (no uptake on scan, progression despite RAI, or cumulative >600 mCi)

nodes.criteria: RAI-refractory, progressive/symptomatic, or threatening disease

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Enrollment in clinical trial is encouraged (PAP-10).

options.label: Systemic therapy (see Principles of Systemic Therapy)
options.recommendation: Preferred: Lenvatinib (category 1). For NTRK fusions: entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib. For RET fusions: pralsetinib, selpercatinib. Useful in certain circumstances: sorafenib (category 1), cabozantinib after progression on lenvatinib/sorafenib (category 1 for papillary), dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, pembrolizumab for TMB-H or MSI-H/dMMR (THYR-B 1 of 5).

options.label: Resection of distant metastases and/or RT/other local therapies
options.recommendation: For progressive and/or symptomatic metastatic lesions (PAP-10).

options.label: EBRT for local symptoms
options.recommendation: For bone metastases or CNS lesions (PAP-11, PAP-12).

adjuvant_logic.summary: TSH suppression with levothyroxine should be continued (target based on risk). Best supportive care per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines.

nodes.criteria: Symptomatic or asymptomatic in weight-bearing sites

options.label: Surgical palliation and/or RT/other local therapies
options.recommendation: Embolization prior to surgical resection to reduce hemorrhage risk (PAP-11).

options.label: Embolization or other interventional procedures
options.recommendation: Can be used as alternatives to surgery/RT in select cases (PAP-11).

options.label: Intravenous bisphosphonate or denosumab
options.recommendation: Caution for hypocalcemia in patients with hypoparathyroidism or vitamin D deficiency; discontinuing denosumab can cause rebound fractures (PAP-11).

options.label: Systemic therapy for progressive/symptomatic disease
options.recommendation: As per THYR-B (PAP-11).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Disease monitoring may be appropriate for asymptomatic indolent disease. Best supportive care.

nodes.criteria: Solitary CNS lesion

options.label: Neurosurgical resection
options.recommendation: For solitary lesions (PAP-12).

options.label: Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS)
options.recommendation: Alternative to surgery (PAP-12).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider systemic therapy for progressive/symptomatic disease. Best supportive care.

nodes.criteria: Multiple CNS lesions

options.label: Radiotherapy (WBRT or SRS) ± resection in select cases
options.recommendation: WBRT or SRS; resection for acute decompression (PAP-12).

options.label: Systemic therapy for progressive/symptomatic disease
options.recommendation: As per THYR-B (PAP-12).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Best supportive care.

management_pathways.title: Follicular Carcinoma – Post-Lobectomy Results
branching_basis: Final pathology after lobectomy for follicular neoplasm (Bethesda IV)

nodes.criteria: Benign or NIFTP

options.label: Levothyroxine to keep TSH normal
options.recommendation: No further surgery needed; ongoing surveillance (FOLL-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No RAI. Disease monitoring as per FOLL-6.

nodes.criteria: Papillary carcinoma

options.label: See PAP-3
options.recommendation: Follow papillary carcinoma pathway (FOLL-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: As per papillary guidelines.

nodes.criteria: Invasive follicular carcinoma (widely invasive or angioinvasive with ≥4 vessels)

options.label: Completion thyroidectomy
options.recommendation: Prompt completion thyroidectomy (FOLL-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Postsurgical evaluation includes TSH, Tg, TgAb, and RAI considerations per FOLL-3.

nodes.criteria: Encapsulated angioinvasive with <4 vessels, or Minimally invasive FTC

options.label: Completion thyroidectomy or disease monitoring
options.recommendation: Disease monitoring is preferred in most circumstances; completion thyroidectomy may be appropriate in selected scenarios (FOLL-1A).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider levothyroxine to keep TSH low or normal. Disease monitoring per FOLL-6.

management_pathways.title: Follicular Carcinoma – RAI Selection after Total Thyroidectomy
branching_basis: Clinicopathologic factors: tumor size, vascular invasion, ETE, lymph node metastases, postoperative Tg, etc.

nodes.criteria: RAI not typically recommended: all present (tumor ≤4 cm, intrathyroidal, no vascular invasion, clinical N0, unstimulated Tg <1 ng/mL, negative US, no TgAb)

options.label: Disease monitoring
options.recommendation: Follow FOLL-6. No RAI (FOLL-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: TSH suppression to normal range.

nodes.criteria: RAI selectively recommended: any of large primary tumor size, minor vascular invasion (<4 foci), cervical lymph node metastases (millimetric central), postoperative Tg 1-10 ng/mL, microscopic positive margins

options.label: RAI therapy
options.recommendation: Empiric dose 30-50 mCi for remnant ablation (if low risk) or 75-150 mCi for adjuvant therapy (FOLL-3, FOLL-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI considered based on clinicopathologic features. TSH suppression per risk.

nodes.criteria: RAI recommended: differentiated high-grade carcinoma, significant N1b, gross ETE, extensive vascular invasion (≥4 foci), postoperative Tg >10 ng/mL, bulky or >5 positive lymph nodes, known/suspected distant metastases, gross residual disease

options.label: RAI therapy
options.recommendation: Dose 100-200 mCi. Consider dosimetry. Evaluate for resectable structural disease before RAI (FOLL-3, FOLL-4, FOLL-5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI recommended. TSH suppression to <0.1 mU/L for high-risk.

management_pathways.title: Oncocytic Carcinoma (Hurthle Cell) – Management
branching_basis: Clinical and radiographic features: evidence of locoregional spread vs. reassuring features

nodes.criteria: Benign or minimally invasive oncocytic carcinoma (encapsulated, no vascular invasion) after diagnostic lobectomy

options.label: Disease monitoring
options.recommendation: Completion thyroidectomy may be appropriate in certain scenarios; monitoring is preferred (ONC-1). Levothyroxine to keep TSH low or normal.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No RAI typically. Follow ONC-6.

nodes.criteria: Invasive oncocytic carcinoma (widely invasive or angioinvasive with ≥4 vessels) after lobectomy

options.label: Completion thyroidectomy
options.recommendation: Then proceed to postsurgical evaluation (ONC-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI considerations per ONC-3. Note: majority of oncocytic carcinomas are non–iodine-avid. Postsurgical evaluation includes neck CT, chest CT, FDG-PET if indicated.

nodes.criteria: Gross residual disease in neck after thyroidectomy

options.label: Resect if possible
options.recommendation: If unresectable, for locoregional invasive disease or rapid progression, consider EBRT or systemic therapy (THYR-B 1 of 5) (ONC-2).

options.label: EBRT
options.recommendation: For threatening vital structures or if RAI absent (ONC-2).

options.label: RAI therapy if uptake present
options.recommendation: If RAI uptake present on imaging (ONC-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: TSH suppression. Disease monitoring per ONC-6/7.

management_pathways.title: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma – Sporadic
branching_basis: FNA or molecular analysis suggests medullary thyroid cancer
Screening for pheochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism, germline RET PV

nodes.criteria: Confirmed MTC, no clinical evidence of pheochromocytoma

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection (level VI)
options.recommendation: Therapeutic ipsilateral or bilateral modified neck dissection for clinically/radiologically identifiable disease (levels II-V). Consider prophylactic ipsilateral modified neck dissection for high-volume or gross central disease. Lobectomy can be considered in select cases without RET PV and no contralateral nodules (MEDU-1).

options.label: Therapeutic EBRT
options.recommendation: Consider only for grossly incomplete tumor resection when additional surgery is not possible. Adjuvant EBRT is rarely recommended (MEDU-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Postoperative levothyroxine to normalize TSH (not suppress). Calcitonin and CEA monitoring at 2-3 months postop (MEDU-5).

management_pathways.title: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma – Germline RET PV (MEN2A/2B/FMTC)
branching_basis: Identification of germline RET pathogenic variant through screening or family history

nodes.criteria: MEN2B (RET PV, typically M918T or A883F)

options.label: Total thyroidectomy during childhood or at diagnosis
options.recommendation: Surgery recommended during first year of life for M918T; before age 5 for A883F. Therapeutic neck dissection guided by imaging. Consider prophylactic central neck dissection (MEDU-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Postoperative levothyroxine to normalize TSH. Surveillance per MEDU-5.

nodes.criteria: MEN2A/FMTC – No primary hyperparathyroidism

options.label: Risk-adapted surgery based on mutation
options.recommendation: Highest-risk (M918T) and high-risk (codon 634, A883F): surgery before age 5 based on calcitonin. Moderate-risk (all other PVs): prophylactic thyroidectomy may be delayed if annual calcitonin normal, US normal, no aggressive family history. Low-risk (codon V804M): surveillance with annual calcitonin and US may be viable (MEDU-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: If surgery chosen: total thyroidectomy and lymph node dissection based on US and calcitonin. Postop levothyroxine to normalize TSH.

nodes.criteria: MEN2A/FMTC – With primary hyperparathyroidism

options.label: Total thyroidectomy + parathyroid exploration
options.recommendation: If single adenoma: excise. If multiglandular: autotransplant one normal parathyroid or leave equivalent mass. Consider cryopreservation (MEDU-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same as above for thyroid management.

management_pathways.title: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma – Recurrent or Persistent Locoregional Disease or Distant Metastases

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence, resectable

options.label: Surgical resection (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preoperative vocal cord assessment if central neck recurrence. EBRT only for unresectable disease not amenable to targeted therapy (MEDU-6).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider systemic therapy for unresectable symptomatic/progressing disease per RECIST. Increasing biomarkers alone are not indication for systemic therapy.

nodes.criteria: Asymptomatic distant metastases

options.label: Disease monitoring
options.recommendation: Consider resection if possible. Systemic therapy if not resectable and progressing by RECIST (MEDU-7).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No urgent treatment. Consider clinical trial.

nodes.criteria: Symptomatic disease or progression

options.label: Systemic therapy (Principles of Systemic Therapy) or clinical trial
options.recommendation: Preferred: selpercatinib (category 1) for RET PV-positive. Other recommended: cabozantinib (category 1), vandetanib (category 1). Useful in certain circumstances: lutenium dotatate (SSTR+), pralsetinib (category 2B) for RET PV-positive (THYR-B 2 of 5).

options.label: EBRT for local symptoms
options.recommendation: For bone metastases or other symptomatic sites (MEDU-7).

options.label: Intravenous bisphosphonate or denosumab for bone metastases
options.recommendation: Caution for hypocalcemia (MEDU-7).

options.label: Palliative resection, ablation (RFA, embolization), other regional treatment
options.recommendation: For selected cases (MEDU-7).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Best supportive care per NCCN Palliative Care.

management_pathways.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma – Stage IVA or IVB (Locoregional Disease)
branching_basis: Resectability assessment (potentially curable with surgery vs. borderline/unresectable)

nodes.criteria: Resectable (potentially curable with surgery)

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic lymph node dissection
options.recommendation: If R0/R1 achieved (often incidentally discovered, very small ATC): adjuvant EBRT with radiosensitizing chemotherapy. If R2 resection: EBRT with chemotherapy (ANAP-2).

options.label: Neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutated disease
options.recommendation: May be considered for borderline resectable disease to facilitate surgery (ANAP-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant EBRT with radiosensitizing chemotherapy (THYR-B 3 of 5) when clinically appropriate. For R0/R1: consider adjuvant chemoradiation; for R2: EBRT with chemotherapy. Disease monitoring after treatment (ANAP-3).

nodes.criteria: Unresectable or borderline resectable

options.label: Targeted neoadjuvant therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E, pralsetinib/selpercatinib for RET fusion, entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusion)
options.recommendation: For borderline resectable disease when safe. Surgery can be reconsidered after response (ANAP-2).

options.label: EBRT with chemotherapy
options.recommendation: When clinically appropriate (ANAP-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: If no surgery, continue chemoradiation ± systemic therapy. Disease monitoring per ANAP-3.

management_pathways.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma – Stage IVC (Metastatic Disease)

nodes.criteria: Stage IVC, aggressive therapy candidate

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic lymph node dissection if resectable (R0/R1)
options.recommendation: Tracheostomy only if strongly indicated, avoid prophylactic tracheostomy. Aggressive therapy ± steroid (ANAP-3).

options.label: Locoregional RT + systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy ± RT (Principles of Systemic Therapy). Molecular testing for actionable alterations if not previously done (ANAP-3).

options.label: Palliative intent
options.recommendation: Palliative locoregional RT, local lesion control with surgery or RT for bone/brain metastases, consider tracheostomy, best supportive care (ANAP-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Disease monitoring if NED achieved. Otherwise, continued palliative care. Consider clinical trials.

items.text: Thyroid and neck ultrasound (including central and lateral cervical nodal compartments) if not previously done [PAP-1, FOLL-1, ONC-1]
items.recommendation: Essential for all patients with thyroid nodule or suspected thyroid cancer.

items.text: Consider neck CT/MRI with contrast when suspicious nodes in the neck detected by ultrasound and/or vocal cord paresis [PAP-1]
items.recommendation: Iodinated contrast is required for optimal cervical CT; potential delay in RAI treatment will not cause harm.

items.text: Consider assessment of vocal cord mobility (ultrasound, mirror indirect laryngoscopy, or fiberoptic laryngoscopy) [PAP-1]
items.recommendation: Especially if abnormal voice, surgical history involving recurrent laryngeal nerve, invasive disease, or bulky central neck disease.

items.text: FNA for suspicious lateral neck nodes; Tg washout useful if cytology negative [PAP-1]
items.recommendation: FNA of suspicious lymph nodes; consider Tg washout of needle aspirate.

items.text: Screen for personal and/or family history suggestive of inherited cancer risk [PAP-1, FOLL-1, ONC-1]
items.recommendation: History of radiation, familial syndromes (Cowden, FAP, MEN2, DICER1).

items.text: TSH, Tg, TgAb measurement (postoperative baseline) [PAP-3, FOLL-2]
items.recommendation: Obtain 6-12 weeks after total thyroidectomy. Tg values used for RAI decision-making.

items.text: Iodine-123 or iodine-131 total body radioiodine imaging (category 2B) [PAP-3, FOLL-2, ONC-2]
items.recommendation: Consider after thyroidectomy if RAI planned; iodine-123 preferred to avoid stunning.

items.text: Neck CT or MRI with contrast for gross residual disease in neck [PAP-3, FOLL-2, ONC-2]
items.recommendation: Evaluate resectability and extent of residual disease.

items.text: Chest CT (non-contrast if suspicion of pulmonary metastases) [PAP-6, FOLL-5]
items.recommendation: If suspicion of distant metastases or elevated Tg.

items.text: FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI as clinically indicated [ONC-2]
items.recommendation: For oncocytic carcinoma or when RAI-negative but high Tg.

items.text: Molecular analysis to identify actionable somatic alterations (ALK, NTRK1/2/3, BRAF, RET gene fusions) [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9]
items.recommendation: For advanced, progressive, or threatening disease. Also consider dMMR, MSI, TMB in select patients. Testing on tumor preferred; ctDNA only if tumor sample unavailable.

items.text: Brain imaging (CT or MRI) with contrast prior to starting systemic therapy [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9]
items.recommendation: Baseline evaluation before TKI therapy due to bleeding risk.

items.text: Basal serum calcitonin level and CEA [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: Essential for diagnosis and surveillance.

items.text: Pheochromocytoma screening (plasma or urinary metanephrines) [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: Mandatory before surgery; treat pheochromocytoma first.

items.text: Serum calcium to screen for hyperparathyroidism [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: Assess for MEN2A-related hyperparathyroidism.

items.text: Screen for germline RET PV (exons 8, 10, 11, 13-16); genetic counseling may be indicated [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: All patients with MTC should have germline RET testing. Do not delay surgery for results.

items.text: Consider additional cross-sectional imaging if calcitonin >300 pg/mL (contrast-enhanced CT neck/chest, liver MRI or 3-phase CT) [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: To evaluate for distant metastases.

items.text: Consider Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT; if not available, consider bone scan and/or whole body MRI [MEDU-1]
items.recommendation: For staging and somatostatin receptor status.

items.text: Postoperative serum calcitonin and CEA level [MEDU-2, MEDU-5]
items.recommendation: Obtain 2-3 months postoperatively to determine residual disease.

items.text: Complete blood count with differential, comprehensive chemistry, TSH [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Routine labs.

items.text: Neck ultrasound; CT with contrast of head, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Accurate staging and assessment of disease extent.

items.text: Laryngoscopy including evaluation of vocal cord mobility; if airway invasion, bronchoscopy [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Assess airway involvement.

items.text: FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Metabolic staging.

items.text: Molecular testing for actionable alterations (BRAF, NTRK1/2/3, ALK, RET); BRAF IHC recommended for faster turnaround [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: BRAF IHC is recommended due to speed. Also consider MSI, dMMR, TMB.

items.text: Brain MRI with contrast [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Identify CNS metastases.

items.text: Expedient consultation with multidisciplinary management team; discuss prognosis and treatment risks/benefits; discuss palliative care options including airway management and IV steroids [ANAP-1]
items.recommendation: Initiate early and often.

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary treatment for most thyroid carcinomas, aiming for complete resection of the primary tumor and involved lymph nodes with preservation of function. For differentiated thyroid cancer, surgery can be lobectomy (for low-risk tumors) or total thyroidectomy (for higher risk). For medullary carcinoma, total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is standard. For anaplastic carcinoma, surgery is considered if resectable, often after neoadjuvant targeted therapy.
principles: Preoperative vocal cord assessment is imperative in patients with voice changes, invasive disease, or bulky central neck disease [PAP-1].
Routine prophylactic central neck dissection is not indicated in most papillary thyroid cancers [PAP-1].
For clinically apparent or biopsy-proven nodal metastases, therapeutic compartmental neck dissection should be performed (central levels VI, VII; lateral levels II-V as indicated) [PAP-1].
Selective dissection of individual nodal metastases (cherry picking) is not considered adequate surgery for nodal disease in a previously undissected field [MS-25].
Completion thyroidectomy has a complication rate similar to that of total thyroidectomy [MS-14].
Lobectomy alone is adequate for papillary microcarcinoma (≤1 cm) without risk factors [MS-13].

Total thyroidectomy is recommended for patients with any of: known distant metastases, extrathyroidal extension, lateral cervical lymph node metastases or gross central node metastases, poorly differentiated or high-grade histology, prior radiation exposure (category 2B), bilateral nodularity or tumors >4 cm [PAP-1].

For follicular and oncocytic neoplasms, diagnostic lobectomy is the initial surgery. Completion thyroidectomy is required if invasive cancer (widely invasive or angioinvasive with ≥4 vessels) is found [FOLL-1, ONC-1].
For medullary carcinoma, total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is standard. Lobectomy can be considered in select cases without RET PV if no contralateral nodules [MEDU-1].

For anaplastic carcinoma, total thyroidectomy with therapeutic lymph node dissection is considered for potentially curable disease. Tracheostomy only if strongly indicated (avoid prophylactic) [ANAP-2].
Risk of hypoparathyroidism and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury is higher after total thyroidectomy; complication rates are lower when performed by high-volume surgeons (>100 per year) [MS-14].

procedures.name: Lobectomy + isthmusectomy
procedures.approach: Removal of one thyroid lobe and isthmus
procedures.indications: Low-risk papillary carcinoma (tumor 1-4 cm, no ETE, no LN metastases, no prior radiation), follicular or oncocytic neoplasm for diagnosis, minimally invasive follicular/oncocytic carcinoma, NIFTP, small papillary microcarcinoma (≤1 cm) with low-risk features.
procedures.key_complications: Hypothyroidism (if contralateral lobe insufficient), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (rare), hypoparathyroidism (rare if unilateral surgery).

procedures.name: Total thyroidectomy
procedures.indications: Papillary carcinoma with high-risk features (ETE, LN metastases, >4 cm, aggressive histology, known distant metastases, prior radiation), bilateral nodularity, medullary carcinoma, anaplastic carcinoma (if resectable), completion thyroidectomy for invasive follicular/oncocytic carcinoma.
procedures.key_complications: Permanent hypoparathyroidism (~1.1%), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (~3.4% long-term), superior laryngeal nerve injury (underreported), transient hypocalcemia (common) [MS-14].

procedures.name: Central neck dissection (level VI)
procedures.approach: Removal of pretracheal, paratracheal, and prelaryngeal nodes
procedures.indications: Therapeutic for clinically apparent/biopsy-proven central nodal metastases. Prophylactic not recommended for most papillary cancers. Routine for medullary carcinoma.
procedures.key_complications: Increased risk of hypoparathyroidism and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury.

procedures.name: Modified radical neck dissection (levels II-V)
procedures.approach: Removal of lateral neck nodal levels II, III, IV, V ± I
procedures.indications: Therapeutic for lateral neck nodal metastases from any thyroid carcinoma. Prophylactic ipsilateral dissection may be considered for medullary carcinoma with high-volume central disease.
procedures.key_complications: Accessory nerve injury (shoulder dysfunction), spinal accessory nerve palsy, chyle leak (if level IV/V dissection).

procedures.name: Prophylactic thyroidectomy (for germline RET carriers)
procedures.approach: Total thyroidectomy before development of MTC
procedures.indications: MEN2B: first year of life for M918T mutation; before age 5 for A883F. MEN2A: surgery before age 5 for highest-risk/high-risk mutations; may be delayed for moderate/low-risk mutations with normal calcitonin and US.
procedures.key_complications: As per total thyroidectomy, but in young children increased difficulty; referral to experienced pediatric thyroid surgeon advised [MEDU-3].

surgery.resectability_criteria: For anaplastic carcinoma, resectability depends on extent of involved structures, potential morbidity and mortality of resection. In most cases, debulking surgery is not indicated. Borderline resectable tumors may be considered for neoadjuvant therapy [ANAP-2]. For differentiated and medullary cancers, resectability is assessed based on imaging and intraoperative findings; gross residual disease after surgery should be resected if possible [PAP-3, ONC-2].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy for thyroid carcinoma includes radioactive iodine (RAI) for differentiated thyroid cancer and external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) for selected cases of differentiated, medullary, and anaplastic carcinoma. RAI is used for remnant ablation, adjuvant therapy, and treatment of known disease. EBRT is used for unresectable gross residual disease, threatening vital structures, palliation of metastases, and as adjuvant therapy for anaplastic carcinoma.
principles: RAI preparation can be via thyroid hormone withdrawal (4-8 weeks for TSH >30 mU/L) or thyrotropin alfa stimulation. Both are acceptable for distant metastases [THYR-C 1 of 5].

An iodine-restricted diet is recommended for 7-14 days prior to RAI. Iodinated contrast should be avoided for 2 months prior. 24-hour urine iodine may confirm adequate restriction (target <50 mcg) [THYR-C 1 of 5].
Negative pregnancy test is required before RAI in reproductive-age patients. Breastfeeding should be ceased 2-6 months prior and permanently after RAI [THYR-C 2 of 5].

Post-treatment whole body iodine-131 scan should be performed in all cases. Pre-treatment diagnostic scan may be considered but can cause stunning (prevent with iodine-123 or low-dose iodine-131) [THYR-C 1 of 5].
Cumulative lifetime activity >1000 mCi requires monitoring for myelosuppression and long-term toxicities [THYR-C 1 of 5].
For EBRT, conformal techniques (IMRT, SIB) are strongly encouraged to reduce toxicity. Pre-treatment imaging (CT, MRI, iodine scan, PET) guides volumes [THYR-C 3 of 5].
For anaplastic carcinoma, RT should start as quickly as possible after diagnosis or within 2-3 weeks postoperatively for adjuvant therapy [THYR-C 3 of 5].

Treatment volumes for differentiated/medullary cancer: GTV includes gross residual disease; CTV includes thyroid bed and involved lymph node levels II-VI; PTV margin 0.3-0.5 cm. For anaplastic: GTV includes primary and involved nodes; high-risk CTV includes involved regions and postoperative bed [THYR-C 3 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: RAI remnant ablation (low-risk)
dose_frameworks.indication: Low-risk DTC (T1b/T2, N0, no adverse features). Category 1 for 30-50 mCi. Also category 2B for T1b/T2 with small-volume N1a (<5 LN mets <2 mm) and primary <4 cm, M0 with minor ETE [THYR-C 2 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: RAI adjuvant therapy
dose_frameworks.indication: Higher likelihood of residual disease based on operative pathology or pretherapy radioiodine scan [THYR-C 2 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: RAI treatment of known disease
dose_frameworks.schedule: Single dose; dosimetry can be used to determine maximal safe dose
dose_frameworks.indication: Proven unresectable or metastatic disease. Higher doses for large-volume disease. For diffuse lung metastases, 48-h whole-body dose should not exceed ~80 mCi to avoid pulmonary fibrosis; bone marrow retention max ~120 mCi at 48 h [THYR-C 2 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: EBRT for differentiated/medullary adjuvant (after R1 resection)
dose_frameworks.indication: High-risk disease after incomplete resection (microscopic residual). Elective nodal regions: 50-56 Gy [THYR-C 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: EBRT for differentiated/medullary salvage (R2 or inoperable)
dose_frameworks.indication: Gross residual disease or inoperable patients [THYR-C 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: EBRT for anaplastic adjuvant (R0/R1 resection)
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 1.2 Gy BID or 1.8-2 Gy daily for microscopic; 0.8-1.0 Gy BID or 1.6-1.8 Gy daily for elective nodes
dose_frameworks.schedule: Twice-daily or once-daily fractions. Chemoradiation may be considered on individual basis [THYR-C 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: EBRT for anaplastic salvage (R2 or inoperable)
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 1.2 Gy BID or 1.8-2 Gy daily for gross; 1.2 Gy BID or 1.8-2 Gy daily for microscopic; 0.8-1.0 Gy BID or 1.6-1.8 Gy daily for elective
dose_frameworks.schedule: Twice-daily or once-daily. Chemoradiation may be considered [THYR-C 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT for bone/soft tissue metastases (all histologies)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 8 Gy (single fraction); 20 Gy/5 fractions; 30 Gy/10 fractions
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 8 Gy x1, 4 Gy x5, 3 Gy x10
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliation for painful metastases. For oligometastases and good PS, consider 45-60 Gy in 1.8-2 Gy fractions or SBRT [THYR-C 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative neck RT for anaplastic
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 20 Gy/5 fx, 30 Gy/10 fx, 45 Gy/15 fx

approaches.name: Postoperative RAI for differentiated thyroid cancer
approaches.dose_fractionation: Single administration of 30-200 mCi based on risk category
approaches.indication: Remnant ablation, adjuvant therapy, or treatment of known disease after total thyroidectomy, based on clinicopathologic risk factors [PAP-4, FOLL-3, ONC-3].
approaches.key_trial: SELECT trial (lenvatinib vs placebo) showed PFS 18.3 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.21) [MS-26]. DECISION trial (sorafenib vs placebo) showed PFS 10.8 vs 5.8 months (HR 0.59) [MS-27]. COSMIC-311 (cabozantinib vs placebo) showed PFS improvement in RAI-refractory DTC after prior VEGFR TKI [MS-27]. Ablation trials: Mallick 2012 and Schlumberger 2012 established 30 mCi for low-risk ablation [MS-16].
approaches.toxicities: Salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (especially if cumulative >1000 mCi), myelosuppression [MS-17].

approaches.name: Adjuvant/definitive EBRT for differentiated/medullary thyroid cancer
approaches.dose_fractionation: 60-70 Gy to gross disease, 50-66 Gy to microscopic disease, 50-56 Gy to elective nodes, all at 1.8-2 Gy/fraction
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: None typically; may consider concurrent doxorubicin based on prospective data (phase II study showed 79.7% LRPFS and 77.3% OS with IMRT ± doxorubicin) [MS-20].
approaches.indication: High-risk disease after surgery (R1/R2), unresectable locoregional recurrence, gross residual disease not amenable to further resection, threatening vital structures [PAP-3, MEDU-1, ONC-2].
approaches.key_trial: Romesser et al 2021: phase 2 trial of IMRT ± doxorubicin showed locoregional PFS 79.7% and OS 77.3% at 2 years [MS-20]. Choi et al 2025: long-term results of EBRT ± chemotherapy in DTC [THYR-C 5 of 5].
approaches.toxicities: Acute: mucositis, dysphagia, dermatitis. Late: xerostomia, esophageal stricture, laryngeal edema, hypothyroidism (if thyroid remaining), spinal cord myelopathy (rare with modern techniques).

approaches.name: EBRT for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
approaches.dose_fractionation: Adjuvant: 60-66 Gy (microscopic) ± SIB to elective nodes 45-54 Gy. Salvage: 66-70 Gy to gross disease. Palliative: 20-45 Gy in 5-15 fractions. Often twice-daily fractionation (1.2 Gy BID) [THYR-C 4 of 5].
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Chemotherapy with radiosensitizing agents (carboplatin/paclitaxel, docetaxel, or doxorubicin) is often used concurrently. Adjuvant chemoradiation is associated with improved survival [MS-39].
approaches.indication: Adjuvant after R0/R1 resection; definitive for unresectable locoregional disease; palliation for metastatic disease.
approaches.key_trial: Pezzi et al 2017: higher RT dose associated with improved OS for unresected ATC (NCDB) [MS-39]. Heron et al 2002: hyperfractionation chemoradiotherapy improved local response to ~80%, median survival 1 year [MS-41].
approaches.toxicities: Significant acute toxicity: severe mucositis, dermatitis, dysphagia often requiring enteral nutrition. Late: fibrosis, esophageal stenosis. Consider obtaining dental, speech/swallow, and nutrition evaluation pre-treatment [THYR-C 3 of 5].

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for progressive or symptomatic disease that is not amenable to surgery or RAI. For differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) that is RAI-refractory, lenvatinib (category 1) is the preferred first-line option; sorafenib (category 1) is an alternative. Cabozantinib is category 1 for progression after lenvatinib and/or sorafenib for papillary carcinoma. For NTRK fusions, entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib are options. For RET fusions, selpercatinib or pralsetinib. For BRAF V600E, dabrafenib/trametinib. Other options include lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, pembrolizumab (for TMB-H or MSI-H/dMMR), and agents like axitinib, everolimus, pazopanib, sunitinib, vandetanib (useful in certain circumstances). For medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC), selpercatinib (category 1 for RET PV-positive) is preferred. Cabozantinib and vandetanib are category 1 options. Pralsetinib (category 2B for RET PV-positive) and lutenium dotatate (for SSTR+ disease) are useful in certain circumstances. For anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC), neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib is category 2B for BRAF V600E-mutated borderline resectable disease. For metastatic ATC, preferred options include dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF V600E), binimetinib/encorafenib (BRAF V600E), pralsetinib/selpercatinib (RET fusion). Ipilimumab + nivolumab is an option for non-RAS tumors. Other recommended regimens include lenvatinib + pembrolizumab. Useful in certain circumstances: carboplatin/pemetrexed, cisplatin/doxorubicin, doxorubicin, nivolumab, NTRK inhibitors, paclitaxel, pembrolizumab, carboplatin/paclitaxel (category 2B), docetaxel/doxorubicin (category 2B). Kinase inhibitor therapy should not be used for stable indolent disease; the pace of progression and quality of life must be considered.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: RAI-refractory DTC (locoregionally invasive, recurrent, or metastatic) – first-line or progressive disease
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase 3 SELECT trial: median PFS 18.3 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.21) [MS-26]. Tumor response rate 65% vs 12% for sorafenib (not directly compared) [MS-26].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Sorafenib (category 1) [MS-27]. For NTRK fusion: entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib. For RET fusion: pralsetinib, selpercatinib.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: RAI-refractory DTC – progression after lenvatinib and/or sorafenib
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cabozantinib (category 1 for papillary; category 2A for follicular/oncocytic)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase 3 COSMIC-311: median PFS 11.0 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.22) [MS-27]. ORR 11% vs 0%.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E (after prior treatment failure), binimetinib/encorafenib for BRAF V600E (after prior treatment failure), lenvatinib + pembrolizumab (if progression on lenvatinib), pembrolizumab for TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) or MSI-H/dMMR.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Medullary thyroid carcinoma – recurrent or persistent locoregional or distant metastatic, symptomatic or progressive
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: LIBRETTO-531: median PFS not reached vs 16.8 months for cabozantinib/vandetanib (HR 0.28) [MS-35-M36]. ORR 69.4% vs 38.8%. LIBRETTO-001: ORR 69% (prior TKI) and 73% (TKI-naive) [MS-35].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib (category 1) [MS-35]; Vandetanib (category 1, requires REMS program) [MS-35]; Pralsetinib (category 2B for RET PV-positive) [MS-36]; Lutenium dotatate (for SSTR-positive) (useful in certain circumstances) [THYR-B 2 of 5].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma – neoadjuvant for borderline resectable stage IVA/IVB
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Subbiah et al 2018: ORR 69%, 12-month OS 43.2% [MS-39-M40]. Wang et al 2019: complete surgical resection achieved in 6/6 initially unresectable patients after neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib, 1-year OS 83% [MS-41].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Pralsetinib or Selpercatinib for RET fusion-positive; Entrectinib, Larotrectinib, or Repotrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma – metastatic (stage IVC) – first-line
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dabrafenib/Trametinib for BRAF V600E; Binimetinib/Encorafenib for BRAF V600E; Pralsetinib/Selpercatinib for RET fusion
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ROAR trial: dabrafenib/trametinib ORR 56%, median OS 14.5 months [MS-40]. Subbiah et al 2021 (ARROW): pralsetinib ORR 60-74% in RET-mutated MTC; also effective in RET fusion-positive ATC [MS-36]. Binimetinib/encorafenib: phase 2 study in metastatic BRAF-mutated thyroid cancer in Japan [THYR-B 5 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (non-RAS tumors only) [THYR-B 3 of 5]; Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab [THYR-B 3 of 5]; Carboplatin/Pemetrexed (if progression after prior treatment) [THYR-B 3 of 5]; Cisplatin/Doxorubicin; Doxorubicin; Nivolumab; NTRK inhibitors (entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib); Paclitaxel; Pembrolizumab; Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (category 2B); Docetaxel/Doxorubicin (category 2B).

key_regimens.name: Lenvatinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, proteinuria, diarrhea, fatigue, decreased appetite, weight loss, hand-foot syndrome, bleeding risk (use caution with CNS metastases).

key_regimens.name: Sorafenib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, fatigue, hypertension, rash, bleeding risk.

key_regimens.name: Cabozantinib
key_regimens.setting: RAI-refractory DTC after progression on lenvatinib/sorafenib (category 1 for papillary); MTC (category 1)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea, nausea, fatigue, hand-foot syndrome, hypertension, bleeding (contraindicated if severe hemorrhage), gastrointestinal perforation/fistula.

key_regimens.name: Vandetanib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: QTc prolongation (REMS required), hypertension, diarrhea, rash, hypothyroidism, photosensitivity.

key_regimens.name: Selpercatinib
key_regimens.setting: RET fusion-positive DTC or RET-mutated MTC (category 1 for MTC; preferred for RET-positive DTC)

drugs.name: Selpercatinib
drugs.dose: 160 mg orally twice daily (adults); pediatric dose weight-based (≥2 years)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, diarrhea, nausea, hypertension, increased ALT/AST, hypocalcemia, bleeding risk.

key_regimens.name: Pralsetinib
key_regimens.setting: RET fusion-positive DTC or RET-mutated MTC (category 2B for MTC; preferred for RET-positive DTC)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, neutropenia, fatigue, constipation, nausea, elevated ALT/AST.

key_regimens.name: Larotrectinib
key_regimens.setting: NTRK fusion-positive advanced thyroid cancer (DTC, ATC, others)

drugs.name: Larotrectinib
drugs.dose: 100 mg orally twice daily (adults); pediatric dose 100 mg/m² body surface area (max 100 mg) twice daily

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, nausea, dizziness, anemia, elevated AST/ALT, weight gain.

key_regimens.name: Entrectinib

drugs.name: Entrectinib
drugs.dose: 600 mg orally once daily (adults); pediatric dose based on BSA

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, constipation, dysgeusia, edema, dizziness, diarrhea, nausea.

key_regimens.name: Repotrectinib
key_regimens.setting: NTRK fusion-positive advanced solid tumors (including thyroid cancer)

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: BRAF V600E-mutated ATC (metastatic or neoadjuvant); DTC with BRAF V600E (after prior treatment failure)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, pyrexia, nausea, chills, rash, arthralgia, hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (dabrafenib).

key_regimens.name: Binimetinib + Encorafenib
key_regimens.setting: BRAF V600E-mutated DTC that has progressed (useful in certain circumstances); ATC (preferred for metastatic)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, anemia, increased CPK, retinopathy (binimetinib), serous retinopathy.

key_regimens.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: RAI-refractory DTC with progression on lenvatinib (useful in certain circumstances); ATC (other recommended for metastatic)

drugs.name: Lenvatinib
drugs.dose: 20 mg orally once daily (or 24 mg based on tolerability)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV every 6 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Lenvatinib continuous; pembrolizumab q3w or q6w
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Combined toxicities: hypertension, proteinuria, diarrhea, fatigue (lenvatinib) plus immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis, endocrinopathies).

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors including thyroid cancer; MSI-H/dMMR tumors (useful in certain circumstances)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV every 6 weeks

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, nephritis, endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency), dermatitis, infusion reactions.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: Oncocytic carcinoma only (useful in certain circumstances for DTC); ATC (non-RAS tumors only, other recommended)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (for 4 doses) then 240 mg IV every 2 weeks or 480 mg IV every 4 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: every 3 weeks x4; maintenance: Nivolumab alone
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Significant immune-related toxicities (colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, dermatitis, endocrinopathies), infusion reactions.

drugs.name: Paclitaxel
drugs.dose: 175 mg/m² IV day 1 (or 60-90 mg/m² IV weekly for radiosensitizing)

key_regimens.name: Lutenium Dotatate (177Lu-DOTATATE)
key_regimens.setting: MTC (somatostatin receptor positive) (useful in certain circumstances)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia), renal toxicity, hepatic toxicity, nausea.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Disease Monitoring
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For patients treated with total thyroidectomy (with or without RAI), initial disease monitoring begins at 6-12 weeks postoperatively with TSH, Tg, and TgAb measurement. Neck ultrasound is performed at 6-12 months. For patients who received RAI, post-therapy whole body iodine-131 scan is performed several days after treatment. Subsequent surveillance intervals depend on risk category and response.
response_logic: Biochemical response: Serum Tg is the main marker. In athyrotic patients, undetectable Tg (<1 ng/mL unstimulated or <2 ng/mL stimulated) with negative TgAb indicates remission. Rising Tg or new appearance of TgAb suggests recurrence [MS-17-M18].

Structural response: Assessed by neck ultrasound, cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI), FDG-PET, or RAI imaging. Structural disease that is stable and non-progressive may not require immediate intervention.
Dynamic risk stratification: Initial risk estimates are modified over time based on response to therapy (excellent, indeterminate, biochemical incomplete, structural incomplete) [MS-12-M13].

For patients after lobectomy: Trending Tg is not recommended due to limited utility. A one-time Tg and TgAb measurement may be considered. If very elevated and without other causes (bilateral nodules, renal insufficiency), further imaging is warranted [PAP-7].
For patients after lobectomy with contralateral nodule meeting FNA criteria or abnormal lymph node: biopsy (with Tg washout if lymph node) [PAP-7].

For patients after total thyroidectomy without RAI: Abnormal imaging and/or rising Tg prompts biopsy and consideration of additional imaging (neck CT/MRI, chest/abdomen CT, FDG-PET, or RAI imaging). Rising Tg or new TgAb requires similar workup [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6].

For patients after total thyroidectomy with RAI: NED (no evidence of disease) is defined by clinical, imaging, and biochemical criteria (undetectable Tg, no Tg antibodies). Neck ultrasound every 1-3 years for 5-8 years then discontinue for low-risk patients. After 10-15 years of no recurrence in low-risk patients, no further monitoring is indicated [PAP-8, FOLL-7, ONC-7].
imaging_recommendations: Neck ultrasound is the principal imaging modality for surveillance, including central and lateral compartments [MS-24].

For patients with rising Tg or new TgAb after total thyroidectomy with RAI, consider additional imaging: neck CT/MRI with contrast, chest/abdomen CT, FDG-PET, or RAI imaging (diagnostic iodine-131 or iodine-123) [PAP-8, FOLL-7, ONC-7].
For anaplastic carcinoma, imaging (CT or MRI with contrast) of brain, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis at frequent intervals. Consider FDG-PET/CT 3-6 months after initial therapy [ANAP-3].

For medullary carcinoma, calcitonin and CEA are the primary surveillance markers. Imaging (neck ultrasound, CT/MRI neck/chest/liver) indicated if calcitonin ≥150 pg/mL or elevated CEA. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT can be used [MEDU-5].

Iodine-131 whole body scan is used for differentiated thyroid cancer to detect RAI-avid disease. Iodine-123 or low-dose iodine-131 (1-3 mCi) is used for diagnostic scans to minimize stunning [THYR-C 1 of 5].
FDG-PET is indicated for patients with negative whole body scan and elevated Tg suggesting structural disease not seen on other imaging [MS-17].
In selected patients at higher risk for residual/recurrent disease (e.g., N1), obtain a stimulated Tg measurement and consider concomitant diagnostic RAI imaging [PAP-8, FOLL-7, ONC-7].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Lesions suspicious for locoregional recurrence should undergo FNA biopsy before salvage therapy. Tg washout is a useful adjunct to FNA, especially if cytology is negative [PAP-9, MS-25].
For biopsy-proven locoregional recurrence: surgery is preferred if resectable. Preoperative vocal cord assessment is recommended for central neck recurrence [PAP-9].

If surgery is not feasible (unresectable): RAI therapy if updated whole body scan shows RAI-avid disease; EBRT for non-avid disease; systemic therapy for progressive disease; local ablative therapies (ethanol ablation, RFA) for limited burden nodal disease [PAP-9].
For iodine-avid locoregional recurrence after prior RAI: repeat RAI therapy may be considered if there is evidence of response [PAP-9].

For distant metastatic disease: RAI therapy if iodine-avid; local therapies (surgery, radiation, ablation) for symptomatic or weight-bearing bone metastases; systemic therapy for progressive/symptomatic non-avid disease [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9].

Active surveillance is appropriate for low-volume disease that is stable and distant from critical structures. Routine intervention for biochemically only (Tg positive, imaging negative) disease is not recommended [PAP-9].
For medullary carcinoma: increasing biomarkers (calcitonin/CEA) alone are not an indication for systemic therapy; structural progression by RECIST criteria is required [MEDU-6].

For anaplastic carcinoma: if NED is achieved after initial therapy, continued disease monitoring with imaging (CT/MRI) at frequent intervals; second-line systemic therapy or clinical trial for progression [ANAP-3].

prognosis.overall: Differentiated thyroid carcinomas (papillary, follicular, oncocytic) generally have an excellent prognosis with 10-year survival rates exceeding 90% to 95% [10,11]. In contrast, anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) is almost uniformly lethal. Mortality rates for thyroid carcinoma are, in general, very low. Differentiated thyroid carcinomas represent more than 95% of all cases, so most thyroid carcinoma deaths are from papillary, follicular, and oncocytic carcinomas. In 2024, it is estimated that approximately 2170 cancer deaths will occur among persons with thyroid carcinoma in the United States [8]. The stable age- and gender-adjusted mortality rate for thyroid carcinoma contrasts distinctly with the declining rates for other solid tumors in adults [32,33].

by_stage.context: 5-year relative survival for stages I to III is about 93% [193,437]
5-year survival for stage IV is about 28% [193,437]
1-year survival rate is about 20% [519,524]; median survival from diagnosis is about 5 months [515,524]
Mean survival of 8 months if disease confined to neck compared with 3 months if extends beyond neck [526]

prognostic_factors: Age at diagnosis is the most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality [112].
Tumor histology, primary tumor size, local invasion, necrosis, vascular invasion, BRAF V600E mutation status, and metastases are important [133-135].
Lymph node metastases are generally associated with worse prognosis, especially in older patients [176-178].
Distant metastases are the principal cause of death from papillary and follicular carcinomas [182,183].
In medullary carcinoma, somatic RET oncogene mutation confers adverse prognosis [140]; calcitonin and CEA doubling times are predictive [440].
In anaplastic carcinoma, older age, distant metastases, WBC ≥10,000 mm3, and dyspnea predict worse prognosis [527-529].
The presence of anti-Tg antibodies and postoperative Tg levels are important for recurrence risk.
Tumor size >4 cm, gross extrathyroidal extension, and aggressive histologic subtypes are adverse factors.
prognosis.prognostic_context: The NCCN Guidelines do not use TNM staging as the primary determinant of management; instead, many characteristics of tumor and patient play important roles. A three-tiered staging system (low, intermediate, high) using clinicopathologic features risk stratifies for recurrence [202-205]. Dynamic risk assessment is also used [206]. The AJCC 8th edition staging may be superior to 7th for predicting disease-specific survival [194].

disease_related.complication: Airway obstruction and suffocation (especially in ATC)
disease_related.management: Tracheostomy only if strongly indicated; prophylactic tracheostomy avoided [525,531]. Airway management and IV steroids discussed early [ANAP-1].

disease_related.complication: Hoarseness due to vocal cord paralysis (from invasion)
disease_related.management: Vocal cord assessment recommended; management per multidisciplinary team.

disease_related.complication: Diarrhea and flushing (from calcitonin secretion in MTC)
disease_related.management: Somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide, lanreotide) may be useful [439].

disease_related.complication: Cushing syndrome (from ACTH production by MTC)
disease_related.management: Treat underlying tumor; consider somatostatin analogs.
Parathyroid exploration during thyroidectomy; autotransplant or cryopreservation [MEDU-4].
Screen preoperatively; resect before thyroid surgery [MEDU-1].

chemotherapy: Cytotoxic chemotherapy has minimal efficacy in differentiated thyroid cancer [311].
For ATC, doxorubicin-based regimens; paclitaxel; carboplatin/paclitaxel; cisplatin/doxorubicin; docetaxel/doxorubicin [THYR-B 3 of 5].
immunotherapy: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab) can cause immune-related adverse events; see NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [THYR-B 1 of 5].
targeted_therapy: Kinase inhibitor therapy is associated with side effects that may significantly affect quality of life; side effects can be fatal [THYR-B 4 of 5].

Common side effects: hand-foot syndrome (sorafenib), hypertension, bleeding, stroke, liver toxicity, proteinuria (lenvatinib), QTc prolongation (vandetanib), severe hemorrhage and GI perforations (cabozantinib) [322,323,362-367,484].
Dose modifications may be required (dose holds, reductions).
Optimal management of dermatologic, hypertensive, and GI side effects is essential; guidelines available [THYR-B 4 of 5].

Denosumab and intravenous bisphosphonates can cause severe hypocalcemia; patients with hypoparathyroidism and vitamin D deficiency at increased risk. Discontinuing denosumab can cause rebound atypical vertebral fractures [PAP-11, FOLL-10, ONC-10, MEDU-7].
surgery: Hypoparathyroidism (transient or permanent) – more frequent after total thyroidectomy [225-229].
Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury – more frequent after total thyroidectomy [225,228,229].
Superior laryngeal nerve injury – under-reported; impacts voice projection [229].
Complication rates lower with experienced surgeons (highest volume: 4.3% overall complication vs. lowest volume: four times more) [230].
radiation: EBRT can cause acute and chronic toxicity; need dental, speech/swallowing, nutrition evaluation pre-treatment [THYR-C 3 of 5].
Radioactive iodine (RAI) therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies [256].
RAI can cause myelosuppression with high cumulative activity (>1000 mCi) [THYR-C 1 of 5].
RAI may induce cerebral edema in brain metastases; high-dose steroids recommended [THYR-C 2 of 5].
Pulmonary fibrosis risk with diffuse lung metastases if 48-hour whole-body dose exceeds ~80 mCi [THYR-C 2 of 5].
In ATC, RT started urgently; chemoradiation generally more toxic [THYR-C 3 of 5, THYR-C 4 of 5].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is essential in thyroid cancer management, especially for advanced/metastatic disease and anaplastic carcinoma. Early initiation of palliative care and discussions about end-of-life care are critical, particularly for ATC where airway management is paramount [520,525]. The NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care should be referenced.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Consider dental, speech and swallowing, and nutrition evaluation prior to RT; gastrostomy placement may be appropriate [THYR-C 3 of 5]. Enteral nutrition may be useful for patients with difficulty swallowing (see NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancer). In ATC, if enteral feeding considered, careful conversation with patient about wishes [THYR-C 3 of 5].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not specifically detailed in the thyroid carcinoma guideline. General antiemetic protocols per NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis should be followed when using chemotherapy.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not specifically detailed in the thyroid carcinoma guideline. G-CSF use per NCCN Guidelines for Myeloid Growth Factors when indicated for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not specifically detailed in the thyroid carcinoma guideline. VTE prophylaxis per NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolic Disease.
supportive_care.pain_management: Bone metastases: Consider surgical palliation, RT, embolization, bisphosphonates/denosumab [PAP-11, FOLL-10, ONC-10, MEDU-7]. Pain management per NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management [THYR-1A, footnote g].
supportive_care.dental_care: Consider dental evaluation prior to RT to determine if pre-treatment optimization of dental and oral health is appropriate [THYR-C 3 of 5].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up after curative treatment for differentiated thyroid cancer depends on initial therapy (lobectomy, total thyroidectomy with or without RAI). For lobectomy: physical exam, TSH, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, consider one-time Tg and TgAb measurement. For total thyroidectomy without RAI: physical exam, TSH, Tg and TgAb at 6-12 weeks, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months. For total thyroidectomy with RAI: physical exam, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, TSH, Tg and TgAb measurement. In selected patients at higher risk, obtain Tg measurement and consider concomitant diagnostic RAI imaging [PAP-7, PAP-8, FOLL-6, FOLL-7, ONC-6, ONC-7]. For medullary carcinoma: postoperative calcitonin and CEA at 2-3 months; detectable calcitonin leads to further imaging; if undetectable, annual serum calcitonin and CEA [MEDU-5]. For anaplastic carcinoma: imaging at frequent intervals as clinically indicated [ANAP-3].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Most recurrences occur in first 5 years, with remaining within 8 years [105]. However, small-volume lymph node recurrences detected by sensitive assays may show little progression over years and not increase mortality [109,110]. Dynamic risk assessment allows modification of initial risk estimates over time [206]. After 10-15 years of no recurrence for patients at low risk, no further monitoring for thyroid cancer is indicated [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6].
late_effects_screening: RAI: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, secondary malignancies (leukemia, salivary gland malignancies) – monitor as clinically indicated [246,256].
TSH suppression: cardiac tachyarrhythmias (especially elderly), bone demineralization (post-menopausal women), symptoms of thyrotoxicosis – counsel on adequate calcium/vitamin D intake [THYR-A].
Kinase inhibitors: monitor for hypertension, bleeding, liver toxicity, QT prolongation, etc. [363,366,416,424].
Post-thyroidectomy hypoparathyroidism: monitor calcium, vitamin D levels.
In MEN2: annual screening for pheochromocytoma and hyperparathyroidism [MEDU-5].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: About 75% of recurrences in differentiated thyroid cancer occur during first 5 years, remaining within 8 years [105]. Recurrences typically located in cervical lymph nodes or thyroid bed [105]. Distant metastases occur in ~3-5% and are principal cause of death [182,183]. Sites: lung (49%), bone (25%), both (15%), CNS (10%) [184-187]. In ATC, most have extensive local invasion or distant metastases at presentation; lungs/pleura most common distant site (≤90%) [518,519].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Differentiated (lobectomy): physical exam, TSH, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months; consider one-time Tg/TgAb. Then annually if NED for 5 years, then every 1-3 years for 5-8 years, then discontinue. After 10-15 years no recurrence, no further monitoring [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6].

Differentiated (total thyroidectomy without RAI): physical exam, TSH, Tg/TgAb at 6-12 weeks; neck ultrasound at 6-12 months. Then annually if stable, neck ultrasound as clinically indicated [PAP-7, FOLL-6].

Differentiated (total thyroidectomy with RAI): physical exam, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, TSH, Tg/TgAb. Then annually if NED; neck ultrasound every 1-3 years for 5-8 years, then discontinue [PAP-8, FOLL-7, ONC-7].

Medullary: if undetectable postoperative calcitonin, annual serum calcitonin, CEA; consider neck ultrasound [MEDU-5]. If detectable calcitonin, CT/MRI as indicated; then calcitonin/CEA every 6-12 months, imaging based on doubling times [MEDU-5].
Anaplastic: imaging (CT or MRI) of brain, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis at frequent intervals as clinically indicated; consider FDG-PET/CT 3-6 months after initial therapy [ANAP-3].
imaging_strategy: Neck ultrasound is principal imaging modality for surveillance of differentiated thyroid cancer [369].
Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI with contrast) when suspicious nodes detected on ultrasound or for vocal cord paresis [PAP-1].
FDG-PET/CT for suspected recurrence with negative iodine imaging and elevated Tg [263].
For medullary: CT/MRI of neck, chest, abdomen with liver protocol if calcitonin ≥150 pg/mL; consider Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT [MEDU-5].
For anaplastic: CT with contrast of head, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis; FDG-PET/CT or PET/MRI; brain MRI [ANAP-1].
laboratory_monitoring: Tg and TgAb: functional sensitivity ≤0.1 ng/mL for Tg, ≤0.9 ng/mL for TgAb recommended [269,270]. Tg should be measured using same laboratory and assay due to inter-laboratory variability [3].
TSH: to guide levothyroxine dose for suppression or normalization [THYR-A].
Medullary: serum calcitonin and CEA are cornerstone [MEDU-5].
Anaplastic: CBC with differential, comprehensive chemistry, TSH [ANAP-1].
supportive_follow_up: See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship for general survivorship care [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6].
Counsel patients with chronically suppressed TSH on adequate calcium and vitamin D intake [THYR-A].
In MEN2 families, genetic counseling and testing for family members [MEDU-3, THYR-E].
For patients with distant metastases, best supportive care per NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care.

special_situations.situation: Active surveillance for low-risk papillary thyroid microcarcinoma (≤1 cm)
special_situations.key_message: Active surveillance is an appropriate management option for selected patients with low-risk papillary microcarcinoma (tumor size ≤1 cm). Limited data exist for tumors >1 cm.
special_situations.management: Neck ultrasound with thyroid and lymph node regions every 6 months for 1-2 years, then annually. Transition to surgery if patient preference, new biopsy-proven lymph node metastases, distant metastases, invasion of recurrent laryngeal nerve/trachea/esophagus, radiologic ETE, or cancer growth by ≥3 mm or 50% volume increase [THYR-D].
special_situations.evidence: Low-quality evidence supports active surveillance for low-risk papillary microcarcinoma [THYR-D]. Studies show risk of tumor enlargement negatively associated with age [113].

special_situations.key_message: RAI should be avoided during pregnancy due to risk of fetal hypothyroidism, malformation, and fetal demise [THYR-C 2 of 5].
special_situations.management: Document negative pregnancy test before RAI. If high doses, consider integrating care with reproductive endocrinology/oncofertility [THYR-C 2 of 5].

special_situations.key_message: Complete cessation of breastfeeding after iodine-131 administration for the current infant.
special_situations.management: Wait 2-6 months after cessation of lactation or with normalization of serum prolactin levels before RAI. No increased risk to mother or infant for subsequent births [THYR-C 2 of 5].

special_situations.key_message: Children typically present with more advanced disease but have favorable long-term survival (90% at 20 years) [114,115].
special_situations.management: Weight-based dose adjustment for RAI; prophylactic thyroidectomy for MEN2B in first year of life (M918T mutation) or before age 5 (A883F mutation) [MEDU-3]. Selpercatinib FDA approved for pediatric patients ≥2 years [THYR-B 1 of 5, THYR-B 2 of 5].
special_situations.evidence: Standardized mortality ratio 8-fold higher than predicted despite good survival [116].

special_situations.key_message: Empiric RAI doses may exceed maximum tolerable activity levels in patients with decreased GFR [PAP-5, PAP-6].
special_situations.management: Special consideration to administered dose and timing with respect to dialysis. Multidisciplinary involvement including radiation safety [THYR-C 2 of 5]. Dosimetry studies considered [PAP-5, FOLL-4, ONC-5].

special_situations.key_message: No study has shown decrease in morbidity or mortality from empiric high-dose RAI in patients with negative imaging [404].
special_situations.management: Active surveillance or RAI therapy with empiric dose ≥100 mCi may be considered; potential long-term side effects may negate benefit [PAP-9, FOLL-8, ONC-8].
special_situations.evidence: Long-term follow-up study showed no survival advantage with empiric high-dose RAI [404].

clinical_pearls: 1. Age is the most important prognostic factor for thyroid cancer mortality; the AJCC 8th edition uses age 55 as cut-off for differentiated thyroid cancer staging, changing from 45 [10,112].

2. BRAF V600E mutation occurs in ~45% of papillary carcinomas; when combined with TERT promoter mutation, it confers worse prognosis [77-81]. However, BRAF V600E alone is generally not considered a poor prognostic factor [82-84].
3. Microscopic extrathyroidal extension alone does not warrant RAI [PAP-4A, FOLL-3, ONC-3].
4. For low-risk papillary microcarcinoma (≤1 cm), active surveillance is an option; patient age is negatively associated with progression [113, THYR-D].

5. In medullary carcinoma, calcitonin and CEA doubling times are key predictors of disease progression; systemic therapy should not be initiated based on biomarker increase alone without structural progression [MEDU-6, MEDU-7].
6. For anaplastic thyroid carcinoma, urgent BRAF IHC testing is recommended due to faster turnaround; dabrafenib/trametinib can be life-saving for BRAF V600E-mutated disease [ANAP-1, 541].
7. After 10-15 years of no recurrence in low-risk patients, no further monitoring for thyroid cancer is indicated [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6].
8. Prophylactic central neck dissection is not recommended in most papillary thyroid cancers; three RCTs showed no benefit in outcomes [PAP-1 footnote f, 377-379].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Thyroid Carcinoma
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 2.2026. Comprehensive guidelines covering nodule evaluation, all histologic types (papillary, follicular, oncocytic, medullary, anaplastic), staging, treatment, and follow-up. Updated annually.

guidelines_resources.title: 2015 American Thyroid Association Management Guidelines for Adult Patients with Thyroid Nodules and Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guidelines for thyroid nodule evaluation and differentiated thyroid cancer management. Also ATA guidelines for medullary thyroid cancer (2015) and anaplastic thyroid cancer (2021) referenced in NCCN guideline [3,430,520].

guidelines_resources.title: ACR Thyroid Imaging, Reporting and Data System (TI-RADS)
guidelines_resources.notes: White paper published 2017; provides recommendations for management of thyroid nodules based on ultrasound findings [60].

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Staging for thyroid-differentiated, anaplastic, and medullary carcinoma. Published 2017. Used in NCCN guideline for staging tables [10].

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens From Patients With Carcinomas of the Thyroid Gland
guidelines_resources.notes: Updated June 2017 to reflect AJCC 8th edition. Provides definitions for vascular invasion, NIFTP, etc. [100].

guidelines_resources.title: ThyCa.org
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides resource information for low-iodine diet preparation [THYR-C 1 of 5]. Also LIDLifeCommunity.org.

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of lenvatinib versus placebo in radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 18.3 months (lenvatinib) vs. 3.6 months (placebo); HR 0.21, 99% CI 0.14-0.31, p<0.001
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Overall response rate 65% vs. 12%; six treatment-related deaths in lenvatinib arm
key_trials.practice_change: Lenvatinib became preferred first-line systemic therapy for RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (category 1)

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of sorafenib versus placebo in radioactive iodine-refractory, locally advanced or metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 10.8 months (sorafenib) vs. 5.8 months (placebo); HR 0.59, 95% CI 0.45-0.76, p<0.0001
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: One treatment-related death; hand-foot syndrome common
key_trials.practice_change: Sorafenib established as a category 1 option for RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer

key_trials.full_name: A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial of cabozantinib for radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer who progressed during or after VEGFR TKI (lenvatinib and/or sorafenib)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: not reached vs. 1.9 months; HR 0.22, 99% CI 0.13-0.36, p<0.0001. At extended follow-up: median PFS 11.0 vs. 1.9 months; HR 0.22, 95% CI 0.15-0.32, p<0.0001
key_trials.practice_change: Cabozantinib became a category 1 option for disease progression after lenvatinib and/or sorafenib

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of vandetanib in patients with locally advanced or metastatic medullary thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic MTC
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: HR 0.46, 95% CI 0.31-0.69, p<0.001; post-hoc analysis in symptomatic/progressive disease: ORR 37% vs. 2% (p<0.001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS data not yet available; QTc prolongation risk requiring REMS program
key_trials.practice_change: Vandetanib became a category 1 preferred option for advanced MTC; access restricted through REMS program

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of cabozantinib in progressive medullary thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with radiographically progressive metastatic MTC
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 11.2 months vs. 4.0 months; HR 0.28, 95% CI 0.19-0.40, p<0.001
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS: 26.6 vs. 21.1 months (not statistically significant; HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.64-1.12, p=0.24)
key_trials.practice_change: Cabozantinib became a category 1 preferred option for advanced MTC

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of selpercatinib versus cabozantinib or vandetanib in advanced RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with progressive RET-mutant MTC (first-line treatment)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: not reached vs. 16.8 months; HR 0.28, 95% CI 0.16-0.48, p<0.001; 12-month PFS 86.8% vs. 65.7%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 69.4% vs. 38.8%; treatment failure-free survival significantly improved; safety profile favorable
key_trials.practice_change: Selpercatinib became a category 1 preferred option for RET-mutant MTC (first-line)

key_trials.full_name: Phase 2 basket study of dabrafenib plus trametinib in BRAF V600E-mutant anaplastic thyroid cancer
key_trials.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology (2018), Nature Medicine (2023)
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced or metastatic BRAF V600E-mutant anaplastic thyroid cancer
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily + trametinib 2 mg once daily
key_trials.key_result: Confirmed ORR 69% (initial); updated ORR 56% (95% CI 38.1%-72.1%) with 3 complete responses; median PFS 6.7 months, median OS 14.5 months; 12-month OS 43.2%, PFS 51.7%
key_trials.practice_change: Dabrafenib/trametinib became a preferred systemic therapy for BRAF V600E-mutated ATC; FDA approved for this indication in 2018
